title: Designing AI Experiences
description: Learn design patterns and UX principles specific to AI-powered interfaces — from managing uncertainty to building trust through transparency.
learningObjectives: Apply proven design patterns for AI-powered interfaces including loading states, confidence indicators, and progressive disclosure
Design graceful error handling and fallback experiences for AI failures that maintain user trust
Build transparency and user control into AI features so users feel empowered rather than replaced

sections.title: Design Patterns for AI Interfaces
sections.content:
AI-powered interfaces break many assumptions that traditional UI design relies on. Responses are non-deterministic, latency varies wildly, and outputs can range from brilliant to nonsensical. Designing well for AI means building patterns that embrace this uncertainty rather than hiding from it.

**Loading States That Communicate Progress**

Traditional spinners tell users "something is happening." AI interfaces need to go further because AI processing often takes longer and produces results in stages.

• **Skeleton screens with contextual labels** — Instead of a generic spinner, show the shape of the expected output with a label like "Analyzing your document..." or "Generating recommendations...". This sets expectations about what's coming.
• **Streaming output** — Show text as it's generated rather than waiting for the complete response. This makes a 5-second generation feel interactive rather than slow. Claude's API supports streaming natively, and most users prefer watching text appear in real time.
• **Progress indicators for multi-step processes** — If Claude is performing several operations (analyzing, then summarizing, then formatting), show which step is active. Users tolerate longer waits when they understand the work being done.
• **Cancelability** — Always let users stop a generation in progress. If someone realizes they asked the wrong question after 2 seconds, forcing them to wait 10 more seconds is disrespectful of their time.

**Confidence Indicators**

Not all AI outputs carry the same level of certainty, and your interface should reflect this.

• **Visual confidence cues** — Use color, iconography, or subtle labels to signal when Claude is highly confident versus making its best guess. For example, a classification result might show "High confidence" with a solid icon or "Needs review" with a caution indicator.
• **Source attribution** — When Claude's output is grounded in specific data, show users where the information came from. This is especially important for summarization or analysis features where users need to verify accuracy.
• **Avoid false precision** — If Claude generates a percentage or numerical score, make sure the interface doesn't present it with more precision than the model actually delivers. Saying "roughly 80% positive sentiment" is more honest than "82.3% positive sentiment."

**Progressive Disclosure of AI Capabilities**

Don't overwhelm users with everything an AI feature can do on the first interaction.

• **Start with the core interaction** — Let users experience the basic AI capability before introducing advanced options like custom instructions, output format preferences, or parameter tuning.
• **Contextual feature discovery** — Surface advanced capabilities when they're relevant. If a user has been generating summaries for a week, suggest that they can customize the summary length or tone.
• **Layered complexity** — Offer a simple mode for casual users and an advanced mode for power users. A "regenerate" button is simple; a "regenerate with these specific instructions" panel is advanced.

sections.title: Managing User Expectations
sections.content:
The biggest design challenge with AI isn't building the interface — it's managing what users expect from it. Users arrive with mental models shaped by science fiction, marketing hype, and their last chatbot interaction. Your design needs to calibrate those expectations quickly.

**Setting the Right Frame on First Contact**

The first moment a user encounters your AI feature determines how they'll evaluate everything that follows.

• **Be specific about what the feature does.** Instead of "AI-powered assistant," say "Summarizes meeting notes and highlights action items." Specificity reduces the gap between expectation and reality.
• **Show examples immediately.** Before users type anything, show them example inputs and outputs. This is the fastest way to communicate what your AI feature does and what quality to expect.
• **Acknowledge limitations upfront.** A brief note like "Works best with English text under 10,000 words" prevents frustration later. Users respect honesty about boundaries.

**Handling the "Wow" to "Meh" Curve**

Most AI features follow a predictable emotional arc: initial amazement, followed by the discovery of limitations, followed by recalibrated expectations. Good design smooths this curve.

• **Avoid demo-quality cherry-picking.** If your onboarding showcases the best possible AI outputs, users will be disappointed by average results. Show realistic examples that represent typical performance.
• **Build in easy recovery from bad outputs.** When Claude produces a poor result — and it will — users need a clear path forward. "Regenerate," "Edit," and "Try a different approach" should all be one click away.
• **Celebrate the right wins.** Help users recognize when the AI saved them time, even if the output wasn't perfect. A summary that needs two edits still saved 10 minutes of writing from scratch.

**Designing for Repeat Usage**

First impressions matter, but retention depends on whether the AI feature is genuinely useful in daily workflows.

• **Reduce friction for common tasks.** If users frequently ask Claude the same types of questions, offer templates, saved prompts, or one-click shortcuts.
• **Show improvement over time.** If your feature learns from user preferences (even via simple heuristics), communicate this: "Based on your previous edits, I've adjusted the tone."
• **Don't force AI into everything.** Sometimes the non-AI path is faster. Give users the choice to skip the AI suggestion and do things manually. The best AI features make themselves optional.

sections.title: Designing for AI Errors Gracefully
sections.content:
AI systems fail differently than traditional software. A database query either returns results or throws an error. Claude might return something that looks correct but isn't, or produce an output that's technically right but unhelpful. Designing for AI errors means anticipating a wider spectrum of failure modes.

**The Spectrum of AI Failures**

Not all AI errors are created equal, and each type needs a different design response:

• **Complete failures** — Claude can't generate a response at all (API timeout, rate limit, content filter). These are the easiest to design for because they're unambiguous. Show a clear error message with a retry option.
• **Partial failures** — Claude produces output but it's incomplete or cut off. Let users see what was generated and offer to continue or regenerate.
• **Quality failures** — Claude responds but the output is poor quality, irrelevant, or incorrect. This is the hardest category because the system "worked" from a technical perspective. Users need easy ways to signal dissatisfaction.
• **Subtle inaccuracies** — Claude generates plausible-sounding content that contains factual errors. This is the most dangerous failure mode because users may not catch it. Design for verification, not blind trust.

**Error Recovery Patterns**

Every error state should offer at least one clear path forward:

• **Retry with the same input** — Sometimes Claude just needs another attempt. Make "Regenerate" prominent and frictionless.
• **Retry with modified input** — Help users understand that rephrasing their request might yield better results. Suggest alternatives: "Try being more specific about..." or "You might get better results if you..."
• **Graceful degradation** — If the AI feature can't perform the full task, offer a simpler version. Can't summarize the entire document? Offer to summarize the first section.
• **Human fallback** — For critical workflows, always provide a path to complete the task without AI. The "Do it manually" option should never be hidden.

**Feedback Loops That Improve Quality**

User feedback on AI outputs serves two purposes: it helps users feel heard, and it generates data to improve the system.

• **Thumbs up/down is a starting point, not a solution.** Binary feedback tells you something went wrong but not what. Add optional follow-up: "What was wrong? Too long / Inaccurate / Off topic / Other."
• **Make editing a feedback mechanism.** When users edit AI-generated content, the diff between the original and edited version is rich signal about where the AI fell short.
• **Close the loop.** If user feedback actually leads to improvements, tell them. "Based on feedback from users like you, summaries now include key dates and deadlines" builds trust and encourages continued feedback.

sections.title: Building Trust Through Transparency and Control
sections.content:
Trust in AI is earned through transparency and control, not through perfection. Users don't need the AI to be right 100% of the time — they need to understand what the AI is doing, verify its outputs, and maintain authority over the final result.

**Transparency Principles**

Transparency in AI design means making the system's behavior understandable without requiring users to understand the underlying technology.

• **Label AI-generated content.** Users should always know when they're looking at content created by Claude versus content written by a human. A subtle "Generated by AI" label or a distinct visual treatment (like a different background color) creates clarity without being intrusive.
• **Explain the "why" when possible.** If Claude recommends an action, surface the reasoning: "Recommended because this customer's sentiment has trended negative over the last 3 interactions." Even a brief explanation dramatically increases user trust.
• **Show what data the AI used.** If Claude analyzed a document, let users see which sections informed the output. If it used their historical data, say so. Users are more comfortable with AI when they understand its inputs.
• **Be honest about what the AI doesn't know.** If Claude doesn't have access to certain context (like data from another system), make that clear. "This analysis is based on the uploaded document only — it doesn't include data from your CRM."

**User Control Patterns**

Control is the antidote to the anxiety users feel when AI makes decisions on their behalf.

• **Review before action.** Never let AI take irreversible actions without user confirmation. If Claude drafts an email, show the draft and let the user send it. If it categorizes support tickets, let users review and override classifications.
• **Adjustable autonomy.** Let users choose how much the AI does automatically. Some users want Claude to draft and send responses. Others want to review every output. Offer a spectrum of automation levels.
• **Undo and revision history.** If AI changes something, users should be able to revert to the previous state. Keep a history of AI-generated versions so users can compare and choose.
• **Customization without complexity.** Let users shape the AI's behavior through simple preferences: "Make summaries shorter," "Use a more formal tone," "Always include dates." These preferences should be easy to set and easy to change.

**The Trust Equation for AI Features**

Trust in AI features follows a formula: Trust = (Competence + Transparency + Reliability) / Perceived Risk. Your design can influence every variable:

• **Competence** — Demonstrate quality through the AI's outputs. Show success stories and highlight what the AI does well.
• **Transparency** — Make the system understandable. Label AI content, show reasoning, surface data sources.
• **Reliability** — Be consistent. Users forgive occasional mistakes but not erratic behavior. If the AI works well for one type of input, it should work well for similar inputs.
• **Perceived Risk** — Reduce the stakes. Give users control, make actions reversible, and provide fallback options. Lower perceived risk means users need less trust to engage.

sections.title: Design an AI-Powered Feature Interface
sections.content: Put your AI UX principles into practice by designing the interface for a Claude-powered feature.

exercise.prompt:
You're designing a Claude-powered feature for a project management tool that automatically generates weekly status reports from task updates, comments, and completed items.

Design the complete user experience for this feature by describing:

1. **First-time experience** — How do you introduce this feature to a user who has never used it? What do you show them before they generate their first report?
2. **Generation flow** — What happens when the user clicks "Generate Report"? Describe the loading states, progress indicators, and how the output appears.
3. **Output presentation** — How do you display the generated report? How do you indicate it was AI-generated? How do you let users verify and edit it?
4. **Error handling** — Design three specific error states: (a) Claude can't generate a report because there's not enough data, (b) the report is generated but seems low quality, (c) the API times out mid-generation.
5. **Trust-building elements** — What transparency and control features do you include? How do users customize future reports?

Be specific about UI elements, copy, and interaction patterns — not just abstract principles.
hints: For the first-time experience, consider showing a sample report generated from example data so users know what to expect before using their own data
Think about how streaming output could make the report generation feel more responsive — users could see sections appear one at a time
For error handling, remember that 'not enough data' is an opportunity to help users understand what data the feature needs to work well
Trust-building doesn't have to be complex — something as simple as 'Generated from 47 task updates and 12 comments this week' shows users exactly what data informed the report
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A thoughtful first-time experience that sets accurate expectations — showing sample output, explaining what data the feature uses, and not overpromising. (2) A generation flow that uses streaming, progress indicators, or staged output rather than a simple spinner. (3) An output presentation that clearly labels the content as AI-generated, shows data sources, and provides easy inline editing. (4) Three distinct error states with specific UI copy and recovery actions — not generic 'something went wrong' messages. (5) At least three concrete trust-building elements such as data source transparency, customization options, or revision history. Deduct points for vague descriptions that could apply to any feature, or for designs that hide the AI rather than embracing transparency.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Designing AI experiences comes down to four core principles:

• **Design for uncertainty, not perfection.** AI outputs vary in quality and latency. Use streaming, confidence indicators, and progressive disclosure to make this variability feel natural rather than broken.

• **Manage expectations actively.** Show users what the AI does well, acknowledge its limitations upfront, and provide realistic examples — not cherry-picked demos. The gap between expectation and reality is where trust breaks down.

• **Make errors a design feature.** AI will fail. Design specific recovery paths for every failure mode — from complete failures to subtle inaccuracies. Users who can recover gracefully from bad outputs will keep using the feature. Users who feel stuck will abandon it.

• **Earn trust through transparency and control.** Label AI-generated content, show what data the AI used, explain its reasoning when possible, and always let users review, edit, and override. Users trust AI features that make them feel in control, not replaced.
